_comment: LENIENT CONFIGURATION: Low threshold for inventive step. Acts as a forgiving EPO Examiner/Applicant. Question titles and leniency tags have been entirely removed to prevent semantic bias.

Q1.text: [Q1] Does the prior art explicitly share the exact same highly specific technical purpose and primary goal as the claimed invention?

Q2.text: [Q2] Does the prior art produce the exact same superior technical effects or unique performance characteristics as the invention?

Q3.text: [Q3] Does the prior art document belong to the precise, exact same specific technical sub-field as the invention?

Q4.text: [Q4] Is there a valid argument that the alleged Common General Knowledge was actually highly specialized or obscure at the time?

Q5.text: [Q5] Is the alleged Common General Knowledge improperly based on highly specific, isolated patent documents rather than broad textbooks?

Q6.text: [Q6] Has the PSA been defined narrowly enough to respect the highly specialized nature of this specific invention?

Q7.text: [Q7] Is the skilled person correctly understood to be completely unimaginative, lacking any creative capability or foresight?

Q8.text: [Q8] Is the skilled person's awareness appropriately limited to standard, basic knowledge rather than obscure academic papers?

Q9.text: [Q9] Is it possible the skilled person would have struggled to find or access these specific, obscure prior art documents?

Q10.text: [Q10] Should the skilled person simply be viewed as a standard individual practitioner rather than an advanced research facility?

Q11.text: [Q11] Has a definitive, undeniably perfect candidate for the 'Closest Prior Art' been isolated?

Q12.text: [Q12] Is the selected CPA undeniably the absolute most promising and explicit starting point, requiring almost no structural changes?

Q13.text: [Q13] Is there an explicit, clear, and direct pointer in the prior art that specifically instructs the skilled person to combine these documents?

Q14.text: [Q14] Do the secondary documents belong to the exact same narrow technical field as the CPA?

Q15.text: [Q15] Would the skilled person have had an undeniable, explicitly documented motive to combine these exact teachings?

Q16.text: [Q16] Is there a perfect, seamless structural compatibility that would guarantee the skilled person could combine these disclosures?

Q17.text: [Q17] Do the distinguishing features provide any technical contribution, context, or real-world utility whatsoever?

Q19.text: [Q19] Do the features completely lack any physical or technical basis, falling entirely into statutory exclusions?

Q20.text: [Q20] Is the problem entirely bypassed without any technical considerations or effects being introduced?

Q21.text: [Q21] Does the implementation involve any technical adaptations, configurations, or engineering effort at all?

Q22.text: [Q22] Is there a plausible technical effect that the feature could be seen to achieve or assist with?

Q23.text: [Q23] Does the invention specify a general real-world application or technical context for these features?

Q24.text: [Q24] Is the application broadly directed toward a technical objective?

Q25.text: [Q25] Are the features generally limited to performing the stated technical purpose in the context of the claim?

Q26.text: [Q26] Is the technical effect at least somewhat surprising, non-obvious, or an improvement over the prior art?

Q27.text: [Q27] Is the technical problem described in reasonable objective terms based on the effects achieved?

Q28.text: [Q28] Was this claimed solution a highly specific path that the skilled person was not inevitably forced down?

Q29.text: [Q29] Is the claimed technical effect generally credible and plausible based on the technical disclosure provided?

Q30.text: [Q30] Does the claim broadly include the main technical features required to achieve the stated effect?

Q31.text: [Q31] Is the description generally sufficient for a skilled person to understand and implement the core invention?

Q32.text: [Q32] Do the features interact to produce a combined effect that provides a useful technical advantage?

Q33.text: [Q33] Do the features generally work together or relate to each other to achieve the result?

Q34.text: [Q34] Can the objective technical problem be reasonably inferred or broadly supported by the original application?

Q36.text: [Q36] Is the claim scope generally supported by the technical contribution? (Answer 'Yes' unless the claim is egregiously broad and speculative).

Q38.text: [Q38] Is it possible the prior art analysis improperly relies on hindsight knowledge of the applicant's brilliant invention?

Q39.text: [Q39] Is there a clear, explicit teaching in the prior art that WOULD force the unimaginative skilled person to make this specific modification with a guaranteed expectation of success?

Q40.text: [Q40] Does the invention boldly accept a disadvantageous modification of the prior art?

Q41.text: [Q41] Was this disadvantage clearly known, thereby deterring the skilled person from proceeding?

Q42.text: [Q42] Did the invention surprisingly yield a technical advantage that compensated for this deterrent?

Q43.text: [Q43] Does the invention relate to biotechnology, requiring specific evidence-based assessments?

Q44.text: [Q44] Does the invention specifically concern the complex field of antibodies?

Q45.text: [Q45] Were the biotech results genuinely unpredictable, avoiding simple extrapolation?

Q46.text: [Q46] Would the skilled person have lacked a concrete, guaranteed expectation of success before embarking?

Q47.text: [Q47] Did the generation of antibodies require any optimization or steps beyond basic known techniques?

Q48.text: [Q48] Did the invention successfully overcome acknowledged technical difficulties or prejudices in the field?

Q49.text: [Q49] Is the invention more than just an obvious, trivial gap-filling exercise?

Q50.text: [Q50] Is the invention more sophisticated than a mere substitution of one identical known equivalent for another?

Q51.text: [Q51] Does the invention leverage material properties in a novel or slightly unexpected way?

Q52.text: [Q52] Is the application of this technique across fields at least somewhat non-obvious or requiring adaptation?

Q53.text: [Q53] Does the invention involve modifications or novel arrangements of known devices?

Q54.text: [Q54] Does the combination of devices provide a new, useful, or mutually beneficial interaction?

Q55.text: [Q55] Does substituting this material into the device provide an unexpected benefit or require special adaptation?

Q56.text: [Q56] Is the selection purposive and advantageous, rather than purely arbitrary?

Q57.text: [Q57] Does the selection of parameters provide a surprising optimization or overcome a difficulty?

Q58.text: [Q58] Does the invention diverge from or improve upon simple, predictable linear extrapolations?

Q59.text: [Q59] Does the specific chemical selection provide an unexpected property or solve a specific problem?

Q99.text: [Q99] Is the problem formulated in a way that respects the invention's true contribution without using hindsight?

Q100.text: [Q100] Can any technical features be identified that differentiate the invention from the CPA?

Q101.text: [Q101] Do the distinguishing features provide a useful technical contribution or context?

Q102.text: [Q102] Is the technical effect at least somewhat surprising or an improvement over the prior art?

Q103.text: [Q103] Is the problem framed reasonably based on the technical effects achieved?

Q104.text: [Q104] Was this claimed solution a specific, non-inevitable path chosen by the inventor?

Q105.text: [Q105] Is the technical effect plausible and generally credible based on the disclosure?

Q106.text: [Q106] Does the claim generally include the main features necessary to solve the problem?

Q107.text: [Q107] Is the description generally sufficient for a skilled person to understand and implement the invention?

Q200.text: [Q200] Can the objective technical problem be reasonably inferred or broadly supported by the original application?

Q201.text: [Q201] Is the claim scope generally supported by the technical contribution? (Answer 'Yes' unless egregiously broad).

Q202.text: [Q202] Does the problem formulation improperly rely on hindsight knowledge of the applicant's brilliant invention?

Q203.text: [Q203] Is there a clear, explicit teaching in the prior art that WOULD force the unimaginative skilled person to make this specific modification?
